data.title: Tether’s potential $20 billion funding round could draw SoftBank, Ark as backers: Bloomberg
data.description:
SoftBank and Cathie Wood's Ark Investment may be interested in investing in a multibillion-dollar funding round for stablecoin issuer Tether, Bloomberg reported Friday.

"The two investors, both longstanding technology financiers, are among several high-profile names who are in early talks to provide funds to the world’s biggest stablecoin issuer in its largest search yet for external capital," the outlet said, citing anonymous sources.

Earlier this week, Bloomberg reported Tether is in talks with investors to raise as much as $20 billion at around a $500 billion valuation, which would make the crypto company one of the most valuable private companies in the world, potentially rivaling the likes of OpenAI and SpaceX.

Cantor Fitzgerald, said to be a Tether shareholder, is advising the potential deal, the report said.

Companies, investors, and advisors will often leak information about potential deals in order to signal interest or gauge market reaction.

SoftBank, Cantor Fitzgerald, and Tether are partners in the Bitcoin treasury firm Twenty One Capital. The company launched with $3.6 billion in bitcoin on its balance sheet, making it, at the time, the third-largest holder of bitcoin among publicly traded companies.

El Salvador-based Tether is the world's largest stablecoin issuer with a supply of $173 billion of USDT tokens. The company recently announced plans to launch a USD-pegged stablecoin for the U.S. market. Bo Hines, CEO of Tether's newly-created U.S. unit, said at a conference in Seoul this week that the stablecoin firm "has no plans to raise money."

Disclaimer: The Block is an independent media outlet that delivers news, research, and data. As of November 2023, Foresight Ventures is a majority investor of The Block. Foresight Ventures invests in other companies in the crypto space. Crypto exchange Bitget is an anchor LP for Foresight Ventures. The Block continues to operate independently to deliver objective, impactful, and timely information about the crypto industry. Here are our current financial disclosures.

© 2025 The Block. All Rights Reserved. This article is provided for informational purposes only. It is not offered or intended to be used as legal, tax, investment, financial, or other advice.

data.title: HDFC Bank’s Dubai branch barred by DFSA from onboarding new clients
data.description:
Private sector lender HDFC Bank Limited on Friday (September 26) said that its Dubai International Financial Centre (DIFC) branch has received a Decision Notice from the Dubai Financial Services Authority (DFSA).

As per the notice dated September 25, 2025, the DIFC Branch has been prohibited from soliciting or conducting any business with new clients who had not completed the onboarding process as of September 25, 2025.

The restriction covers financial services activities, including Advising on Financial Products, Arranging Deals in Investments, Arranging Credit, Advising on Credit, and Arranging Custody.

Additionally, the branch cannot solicit, onboard, or engage in financial promotions with new clients. However, the prohibition does not extend to the continued servicing of existing customers or to onboarding clients who had earlier been offered or provided financial services but were not formally onboarded at that time.

The directive, effective September 26, 2025, will remain in force until amended or revoked in writing by the DFSA. The communication was received by the bank on September 26, 2025, via email.

The DFSA notice cited concerns regarding carrying on financial services for customers not properly onboarded, issues in onboarding processes, and related matters at the DIFC Branch.

HDFC Bank clarified that the DIFC Branch business is not material to its overall operations or financial position, and hence, no significant financial implications are expected. As of September 23, 2025, the branch had 1,489 onboarded customers, including joint holders.

The bank stated that it has already initiated necessary steps to comply with the directives and is committed to working with the DFSA in its ongoing investigation and to remediate and address the regulator’s concerns at the earliest.

Shares of HDFC Bank Ltd ended at ₹945.15, down by ₹4.45, or 0.47%, on the BSE.

data.title: Soy futures flat, headed for weekly loss after China buys Argentina crops
data.description:
Key points:
  • Chinese purchases of Argentine soy loom over US prices
  • US farmers advance soy and corn harvests
  • CBOT corn and wheat futures ease

By Tom Polansek

Chicago Board of Trade soybean futures were unchanged on Friday, headed for a second weekly loss after China made large purchases of Argentine supplies this week at the expense of U.S. crops.

The market was unchanged after falling hard earlier this week on concerns about China's lack of demand for U.S. soybeans. Wheat and corn futures were trading lower.

After Buenos Aires briefly suspended grain export taxes this week, around 40 Argentine soybean cargoes were registered for export in November and December, mostly headed to China, two traders told Reuters. On Thursday, Argentina reinstated export taxes.

Still, the buying frenzy by Chinese importers was a fresh blow for U.S. soybean farmers, who have been shut out of exports to top market China during the current harvest season as trade war tariffs make their beans prohibitively expensive for Chinese buyers.

"That news has been digested and the taxes have been implemented, so soybeans should have seen the bulk of selling from that news item," said Brian Hoops, president of Midwest Market Solutions.

The most-active soybean contract on the Chicago Board of Trade was unchanged at $10.12-1/4 a bushel by 12:25 p.m. CDT (1725 GMT).

When asked if China would purchase U.S. soybeans, a Chinese commerce ministry spokesperson said Washington should remove what China described as unreasonable tariffs and create conditions to expand bilateral trade.

The advancing U.S. soy and corn harvests have put further supply pressure on prices, though doubts over the size of corn yields have lent support to that market.

The U.S. Department of Agriculture is slated to issue a weekly update on harvesting progress on Monday.

"Corn is facing an active harvest weekend ahead with weather turning warmer and drier," Hoops said. "The window should be wide open for corn and soybean harvest."

CBOT wheat was down 8 cents at $5.19 a bushel, and corn dropped 4 cents to $4.21-3/4 a bushel.

data.title: Fed's Bowman says decisive rate cuts needed to offset labor market risks
data.description:
Key points:
  • Bowman urges proactive rate cuts to address labor market fragility
  • Fed's recent rate cut aimed at bolstering job market amid inflation concerns
  • Bowman supports smaller balance sheet, tweaks to Standing Repo Facility

By Michael S. Derby

Federal Reserve Vice Chair for Supervision Michelle Bowman reiterated on Friday that she believes decisive interest rate cuts are needed to ward off rising trouble in the job market.

“Recent data show a materially more fragile labor market along with inflation that, excluding tariffs, has continued to hover not far above our target,” Bowman said in the text of a speech delivered before a gathering of the Forecasters Club of New York.

Citing many months’ worth of data showing mounting issues in the job market, Bowman said it is time for the rate-setting Federal Open Market Committee “to act decisively and proactively to address decreasing labor market dynamism and emerging signs of fragility.”

She said “we are at serious risk of already being behind the curve in addressing deteriorating labor market conditions,” and noted “should these conditions continue, I am concerned that we will need to adjust policy at a faster pace and to a larger degree going forward.”

When the rate-setting Federal Open Market Committee met last month policymakers trimmed their overnight interest rate target range by a quarter of a percentage point to between 4% and 4.25%. While officials are still worried about inflation being above their target, their rate cut was aimed at helping bolster the job market.

One Fed governor voted in favor of a larger rate cut but Bowman joined with her colleagues to support the 25-basis point easing. Bowman had dissented at the Fed’s meeting at the end of July in favor of a rate cut while most officials then favored keeping rates steady.

In her speech, Bowman brushed off fears that President Donald Trump’s trade tariffs are on track to create a persistent inflation problem. She said when tariffs are taken out of the equation, price pressures have “continued to hover not far above our target.”

And although inflation is still over the 2% target, Fed policy should “focus on the side of the mandate that is showing signs of deterioration or fragility,” which means acting to support the job market, the official said.

BALANCE SHEET DRAWDOWN

Bowman also took on the state of the Fed’s ongoing contraction of its bond holdings and said “my preference is to maintain the smallest balance sheet possible with reserve balances at a level closer to scarce than ample.”

Shrinking the balance sheet as much as possible now gives the Fed flexibility to respond to future troubles if needed, the official said. Bowman also said she’d like an all-Treasury balance sheet tilted toward shorter-dated holdings, and noted the Fed could rejigger its holdings toward longer-dated bonds if needed without increasing the overall size of its holdings.

Bowman also said she was willing to go beyond the passive balance sheet runoff the Fed is currently using to reduce its holdings. "I also look forward to revisiting the committee's consideration of potential sales of our agency MBS holdings," Bowman said. "Simply relying on MBS runoff will not allow returning to a Treasury-only portfolio within a credible time frame."

Selling mortgage-backed securities could potentially be problematic for the Fed, as it would be unloading supply into a housing-related market already under stress, amid high rates. Selling that class of securities could make home borrowing more expensive if it was done aggressively.

Bowman also addressed Fed liquidity facilities in her remarks and expressed interest in changes to the Standing Repo Facility, or SRF, a new and largely untested tool the Fed created in 2021 to provide a shock absorber for markets when liquidity ran short. The SRF is expected to see major usage next week when market participants manage liquidity needs over the quarter-end.

“My preference would be for a minimum bid rate higher than the top of the federal funds rate target range in order to emphasize that the SRF’s purpose is to serve only as a backstop,” rather than a normal source of funding, Bowman said.

“A rate above the top of the target range would be more likely to discourage use of the facility outside of exceptional market-wide episodes of acute stress.”

data.title: CardioComm Solutions Inc. Announces Insider Purchase of Third-party Loan
data.description:
Deal Consolidates All Debt Under Insider Control

Toronto, Ontario--(Newsfile Corp. - September 26, 2025) - CardioComm Solutions Inc. ("CardioComm" or the "Company"), a global provider of consumer heart monitoring and medical electrocardiogram ("ECG") software solutions, today announced that its Chief Executive Officer, Mr. Etienne Grima, has purchased from one of the Company's lenders a loan with a principal value of $80,000 (the "Loan").

The Loan represents a portion of original loans totaling $600,000, which were first announced by the Company on December 21, 2016. Over time, the Company has disclosed extensions of these loans in press releases, during which portions of the original debt were progressively retired.

This acquisition constitutes a private asset purchase by the CEO, who has personally assumed the lender's position in the Loan.

As a result of this transaction, all of CardioComm's debt is now held by insiders of the Company. This consolidation of obligations under insider ownership reflects management's continued confidence in CardioComm's strategic direction and strengthens the Company's ability to advance its commercialization plans without reliance on third-party debt providers.

CardioComm reiterates that the Loan terms remain unchanged, and the purchase does not alter the Company's financial reporting obligations. The acquisition of the Loan by Mr. Grima was unanimously approved by the directors of the Company.

Voluntary Disclosure

The Company is providing disclosure of this private transaction on a voluntary basis in the interest of transparency to shareholders. The disclosure also ensures continued compliance with the requirements of Leede Financial Inc., which facilitated as agent and administered the Loan in prior reporting periods.

The Company notes that this transaction is a private asset purchase between the CEO and a third-party lender. The Loan remains on the same terms and conditions as previously disclosed by the Company, and no new securities of CardioComm are issuable in connection with this transfer.

The transaction does not constitute a "related party transaction" under Multilateral Instrument 61-101 and does not trigger a TSX Venture Exchange ("TSXV") review. The transfer represents only a change in beneficial ownership of the Loan, and therefore does not require TSXV approval.

This transaction does not involve the issuance of new securities, material amendments to the debt instrument, or a transaction that would materially affect control of the Company.

Forward-looking statements.

This release may contain certain forward-looking statements and forward-looking information with respect to the financial condition, results of operations and business of CardioComm Solutions and certain of the plans and objectives of CardioComm Solutions with respect to these items. Such statements and information reflect management's current beliefs and are based on information currently available to management. By their nature, forward-looking statements and forward-looking information involve risk and uncertainty because they relate to events and depend on circumstances that will occur in the future and there are many factors that could cause actual results and developments to differ materially from those expressed or implied by these forward-looking statements and forward-looking information.

In evaluating these statements, readers should not place undue reliance on forward-looking statements and forward-looking information. The Company does not assume any obligation to update the forward-looking statements and forward-looking information contained in this release other than as required by applicable laws, including without limitation, Section 5.8(2) of National Instrument 51-102 (Continuous Disclosure Obligations).

Neither TSX Venture Exchange nor its Regulation Services Provider (as that term is defined in policies of the TSX Venture Exchange) accepts responsibility for the adequacy or accuracy of this release.

FOR FURTHER INFORMATION PLEASE CONTACT:

Etienne Grima, Chief Executive Officer

1-877-977-9425 x227

investor.relations@cardiocommsolutions.com

To view the source version of this press release, please visit https://www.newsfilecorp.com/release/268179

data.title: Argentina Treasury taps market to rebuild reserves after agro dollar windfall
data.description:
By Jorge Otaola

Argentine markets rallied at the end of a volatile week, buoyed by a multi-billion dollar influx from agricultural exporters and strong support from the U.S. Treasury.

A temporary tax break spurred the farm sector to commit to selling $7 billion in exports. Separately, U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent announced negotiations for a $20 billion currency swap and pledged broad support for the country.

Argentina's Treasury is taking advantage of the inflow to purchase dollars - a move viewed by the market as a soft currency intervention.

According to traders, the Treasury acquired around $700 million by selling pesos at approximately 1,350 per dollar, though the government did not confirm the move.

In response, the wholesale peso staged a comeback on Friday to trade at 1,330 to the dollar, reversing a sharp decline from the start of the week when it hit a record low of 1,476 and neared the limit set by the central bank.

"The Treasury has already begun rebuilding reserves," said Federico Filippini, an analyst at Adcap Grupo Financiero. "What's more, the decisive support from the United States has stabilized conditions."

The wholesale peso appreciated nearly 11% in the week after slipping some 1.5% the week before, while the S&P Merval stock index was on track for a 7.6% weekly gain to rebound from a 4.3% loss last week.

In contrast, government bond prices fell 1.2% as investors cashed in on recent gains.

Agricultural exporters have until Monday to liquidate 90% of what they've declared.

"The Treasury must take advantage of this temporary window to continue accumulating reserves, as it won't have this opportunity later," said brokerage firm Max Capital.

Traders acknowledge that the past two weeks have been the most frantic in Argentina's financial markets since libertarian President Javier Milei took office in December 2023.

"The market should return to a bullish or optimistic outlook," Adcap's Filippini said, "as investors will assign a higher probability to the country regaining access to international credit markets."

Argentina's country risk (11EMJ) hovered around 1,010 basis points on Friday, down sharply from a peak of 1,500 points a week ago.

The government faces a tough battle in mid-term elections at the end of October, where it will seek to make gains from its current minority position in Congress.

data.title: Ulta Seen in a Better Spot to Weather Competition — Market Talk
data.description: Ulta is in a better spot to face competition as industry dynamics improve while the company makes progress on its internal execution, UBS analysts say in a note. Demand seems to be improving across the beauty industry while promotions appear rational, setting up a supportive backdrop for Ulta to see a high return on investment as it invests further in its product assortment and guest experience, the analysts say. The company is also poised to gain market share as drug and department stores continue to close locations, they say. Such dynamics should further support the stock's outperformance, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: Jobs Report, Nike Earnings: What to Watch Next Week — WSJ
data.description:
By WSJ Staff

The monthly jobs report is due at the end of next week. Softness in the labor market has been the main reason for expected Federal Reserve rate cuts. Here's what to watch:

Monday

  • Economic data: Pending home-sales data for August
  • Fed speakers: New York Fed President John Williams, Fed governor Christopher Waller
  • Geopolitics: The United Nations General Assembly concludes Monday
  • Earnings: Carnival

Tuesday

  • Economic data: Job openings report (JOLTS) for August, consumer-confidence data for September, Home-price indexes for July
  • Fed speakers: Vice Chair Philip Jefferson, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee
  • Earnings: Nike, Paychex

Wednesday

  • Earnings: Cal-Maine Foods, Conagra Brands
  • Economic data: Manufacturing reports

Thursday

  • Economic Data: Weekly jobless claims, August factory orders

Friday

  • Economic data: Nonfarm payrolls for September due at 8:30 a.m. ET. The labor market stalled in August, adding just 22,000 jobs.
  • Fed speakers: Vice Chair Philip Jefferson, New York Fed President John Williams

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Homebuilding Consolidation Helps Make Industry Less Cyclical — Market Talk
data.description: Consolidation in the homebuilding industry has helped make it better able to keep building through downturns, according to John Burns Research & Consulting analyst Jody Kahn. Big builders with significant capital compose 61% of the market in 2024, up from 24% in 2003, a portion that will likely keep growing. These builders can better secure low-cost financing, compete for land and labor and negotiate for lower prices, making the industry less sensitive to demand cycles. "Smaller local builders often struggle during periods of low demand, while larger, capital-backed homebuilders are better positioned to keep building, capitalize on growth cycles, and potentially stabilize the new home sector," Kahn says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1331 ET - Consolidation in the homebuilding industry has helped make it better able to keep building through downturns, according to John Burns Research & Consulting analyst Jody Kahn. Big builders with significant capital compose 61% of the market in 2024, up from 24% in 2003, a portion that will likely keep growing. These builders can better secure low-cost financing, compete for land and labor and negotiate for lower prices, making the industry less sensitive to demand cycles. "Smaller local builders often struggle during periods of low demand, while larger, capital-backed homebuilders are better positioned to keep building, capitalize on growth cycles, and potentially stabilize the new home sector," Kahn says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1319 ET - Speciality beauty and mass retailers continue to beat out drug and department stores in the beauty category, according to UBS in a note. The analysts say they found a sharp discrepancy in sales performance between players like Ulta, Sephora, Walmart and Amazon versus the likes of CVS, RiteAid, Walgreens, Macy's, Kohl's and Nordstrom. Those drug and department stores also continue to close stores, shutting down several hundred in the first half of the year, they say. That should allow room for further market share gains from differentiated players like Ulta going forward, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1309 ET - Okta's security product for AI agents likely won't add any material contributions for the company's growth until fiscal 2028 at the earliest, D.A. Davidson analysts Rudy Kessinger and Andres Miranda Lopez say in a note after attending the company's annual user event. The product will be available in fiscal 2027 and aims to discover risky agents and centrally control agent access, among other functions, the analysts say. Several customers said they are interested in learning more about the offering, but the vast majority are not planning any significant AI agent rollouts anytime soon, the analysts say. Okta management indicates the product will not be a big growth opportunity in the near term, but should be significant in the long term, the analysts say.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1259 ET - KB Home may face obstacles in its path back to build-to-order homes, say Zelman & Associates analysts. Right now, there is an abundance of spec inventory on the market and KB Home's competitors are offering aggressive incentives to offload it. That may make it difficult for the company to compete using its build-to-order offerings, especially as it has adopted the strategy of cutting price rather than offering incentives. "It remains to be seen if KBH is able to differentiate itself as a build-to-order builder focusing on transparent pricing rather than an incentive-heavy pricing approach," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1256 ET - Costco Wholesale delivered solid results in the F4Q, but it's increasingly difficult for the company to meet expectations as it's considered one of the best retailers in the world, DA Davidson analysts Michael Baker and Keegan Cox say. Investors already knew what to expect from sales because of Costco's monthly reporting, but incremental numbers were mixed, Baker and Cox say. Membership fee income beat expectations and gross margins were up as expected, but EBIT dollars were slightly below consensus estimates and sales growth slowed, they say. Costco is off 2.3%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1253 ET - KB Home said it wants to get back to its historic levels of building 70% of its homes to order rather than on spec. That's a worthwhile long-term goal, but its success will depend on strength of demand during the spring selling season, say Zelman & Associates analysts. Historically, the company has built a competitive advantage and higher margins through build-to-order homes. But right now, the company's backlog is down 22% year-over-year, which means it will need significant order growth in the first half of next year. KB Home also has less spec inventory to use as a buffer if demand for its build-to-order homes doesn't pick up.(nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1241 ET - Costco Wholesale's valuation doesn't match its slowing key performance indicators, Roth analyst Bill Kirk says. The retailer's same-store sales growth has been slowing down since it hiked membership fees, and U.S. growth hit a six-quarter low in 4Q. Membership renewal rates are also weakening. Kirk thinks Costco's performance will start to look more similar to conventional food retailers and will struggle to keep gaining as much market share as it has in recent years, as it gets more competition from Walmart and BJ's. "In our view, the valuation discrepancy between Costco and conventional peers is too wide," Kirk says. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1233 ET - Okta's customers appear increasingly interested in its identity governance offerings, D.A. Davidson analysts Rudy Kessinger and Andres Miranda Lopez say in a note after meeting with customers and channel partners at the company's annual user event. Customers shared modestly better feedback on Okta's identity governance and privileged access management products than the analysts observed in recent field work, they say. Okta's identity governance offerings are resonating particularly well with small- and mid-sized businesses, though larger enterprises are still predominantly using competitor SailPoint, they say.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1230 ET - American Airlines won't be able to stop the city of Chicago from awarding rival United Airlines five additional gates at O'Hare, a Cook County Circuit Court judge has ruled. Both airlines claim O'Hare a hub, but American has accused United of trying to squeeze it out and hampering American's expansion plans. It said Friday that the ruling would give United a competitive advantage in Chicago, but said it remains committed to the city. United President Brett Hart told employees that the decision was a "powerful validation of United's growth in the city. (alison.sider@wsj.com; @alyrose)

1212 ET - Nike faces high expectations as it approaches the back half of its fiscal year, limiting the possibility of further stock upside, Raymond James analysts say in a note. Though the company's revenue decline should moderate in its fiscal first quarter report, Wall Street already expects revenue growth to inflect positively in the back half of the fiscal year, starting in the fiscal third quarter, the analysts say. The magnitude of such growth will likely be limited given a high level of promotions and weak wholesale orders in China, among other headwinds, they say. "We don't have strong conviction on the magnitude of the positive inflection in F2H," they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1159 ET - Nike should show further progress in its turnaround strategy in its next earnings report, with potential for revenue to outperform prior guidance, Raymond James analysts say in a note. The analysts say they would not be surprised to see sales down low-single digits, versus the mid-single digit decline the company previously forecast. Nike's new innovation has been well received, and its wholesale and direct-to-consumer businesses are improving sequentially, the analysts say, though several headwinds remain, including rising costs and continued soft demand in China. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

0919 ET - Macroeconomic factors are leading Gen Z consumers to reduce their visits to fast-casual restaurants, causing a slowdown in sales for companies like Cava, Chipotle and Sweetgreen, says Benchmark analyst Todd Brooks. The federal government restarted student loan payment collections in May, while the unemployment rate for recent college graduates also grew to 5.8% in March of this year from 4.1% in March of 2023, according to the analyst. "We believe that these headwinds are leading younger consumers to cut their frequency of visiting their favorite fast casual concepts, as the price points for these visits can often run north of $20," Brooks says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Alaska Air Names Jason Berry as Operating Chief, Constance Von Muehlen to Retire
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Alaska Air named Jason Berry its next chief operating officer.

Berry will succeed Constance von Muehlen, who plans to retire on Feb. 15, the airline said Friday. Berry will start as operating chief on Nov. 3, at which time von Muehlen will become an adviser to help oversee the transition.

Berry will be stepping down as president of Horizon Air Industries, Alaska's regional airline subsidiary based in Washington state. Berry has held that position since 2023, and last year began overseeing Alaska Air's cargo division.

He previously was head of operations and president of McGee Air Services, another Alaska Air entity.

Andrea Schneider will succeed Berry as president of Horizon Air.

Von Muehlen has been chief operating officer since 2021.

Alaska Air plans to pay Berry a base salary of $525,000. Von Muehlen made a total compensation of $3.5 million in 2024.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: AI and Other Tech Are Changing Wealth Management. This Citi Executive Explains How. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Steve Garmhausen

In five years, predicts Jelena Zec, wealth management will look pretty much the same as it does today — from the outside. "I think the human relationship will still be at the heart of it," says Zec, a venture investing director with Citi Ventures. "But the background will be all automated, super-efficient, and highly personalized."

That will be due to the kind of technology that Citi Ventures, the venture-capital business of $2.6 trillion-asset Citi, invests in. Founded 15 years ago, Citi Ventures has 147 portfolio companies, 80 of which are fintechs and 14 of which are solely focused on wealthtech. Speaking with Barron's Advisor, Zec explains how artificial intelligence and the great wealth transfer are driving her business' investment decisions. She discusses portfolio holdings and gives a shoutout to a start-up Citi Ventures doesn't own, one that may exemplify how to serve less-wealthy clients profitably. And she urges those whose businesses AI might disrupt to embrace rather than resist it.

What do we need to know about Citi Ventures? As the name suggests, we're Citibank's venture-capital arm. We've been around for 15 years now, and we've backed around 150 portfolio companies. Our goal is to find those companies that are defining and shaping the future of financial services. I think of us as playing two roles. One is to catalyze innovation at Citi by investing and partnering with these cutting-edge start-ups. The other is to help our portfolio companies by facilitating commercializations, delivering Citi and other financial institutions as customers. We have a pretty good success rate at commercializing our portfolio. I think around 50% of our portfolio is working with Citi in some shape or form. So we invest in everything fintech, and we define fintech quite broadly. It's any technology that touches financial services, and it can be wealthtech, payments, lending, future of commerce. It can be vertical software, cyber, compliance, or fraud.

What are your target companies these days in wealthtech? Wealthtech is one of the investment pieces that we've had for a long time at Citi Ventures. In the past we've backed companies like Betterment, iCapital, DriveWealth, InvestCloud, and Masttro. So we already had a robust portfolio, but with the advancements in AI, which is rapidly becoming the backbone of modern wealth management and is transforming this space, we kind of refreshed the market landscape a couple of years ago and identified two key trends we think will shape where wealthtech is heading. AI is one, of course, and the second, which everybody is talking about, is generational transfer of wealth — I just feel that advisors are not yet really equipped to capture these new clients. So with those two overarching themes, we've mapped the landscape, we've talked to a lot of companies, and we've identified what we think are the category leaders in their prospective areas.

What are some examples of newer wealthtech additions to your portfolio? The backbone of any financial advice is financial planning. We recently invested in a company called Conquest. It was founded by the guys who founded NaviPlan back in the day; they have decided to learn from their mistakes and do it all over again, but with a more tech-forward solution that empowers advisors with gen AI, with smart tools to better devise financial plans for their customers. We've also invested in a company called Wealth.com, which is revolutionizing digital estate planning. Digital estate planning has been overlooked for years: When clients receive their estate plans, they don't even understand half of what it means. But it is essential, and it goes back to my point about the intergenerational transfer of wealth and how can advisors capture clients' offspring as new clients. We think digital estate planning is one of those tools that will help. Wealth.com is leveraging gen AI to speed up the process of abstracting information from existing estate plans, trusts, and wills, as well as quickly create wonderful estate plans that advisors can present to their clients as well as the sons and daughters of their clients.

Digital estate planning is becoming a bit of a crowded space. Why do you think Wealth.com will succeed? We see Wealth.com becoming the leader just because they were able to win incredible logos, incredible customers. I think the winner will probably be the one that forges partnerships with large financial institutions and also stays up to date with technological advancements. That's probably the biggest challenge. Every day I wake up and the world has moved five years ahead. How do you keep up with that? You need the best engineering talent and the best product talent. But you also need a robust go-to-market strategy. I really believe that partnerships with large financial institutions will unlock the market for some of these start-ups and help them become the gold standard. But we're very early in this journey.

What do you think are the biggest pain points AI can address in the wealth management industry? We honestly think that AI is not replacing advisors — it's making advisors more efficient. It's enabling them to deliver highly personalized advice for their customers, and most importantly, it's making advisors more human. Tools like Conquest in financial planning and Wealth.com in digital estate planning are empowering advisors to be more efficient, to deliver highly personalized advice, and to build better relationships with their clients. Another company we've invested in is Jump. It records conversations between advisors and clients, then parses that unstructured data and uses it to enrich [customer relationship management], but also to automate all the tedious tasks in the background — the meeting prep, the email writing.

So if you automate the workflows you're describing, the advisor will have more time to look clients in the eye and talk to them. Yes. I think it unlocks tremendous efficiencies. Advisors spend 50% of their time on administrative tasks. That's time they're not using to advise their clients or acquire new ones. So I think both in the efficiencies and personalization with gen AI can have a tremendous role. I'm going to paraphrase Jump's founder, and say that gen AI is helping us humans to build Iron Man's superpowers and shield. But at core of that is still a human. And human advice and human interaction are what I would say financial advisory is all about.

What impact do you think AI will have on the investment part of wealth management? To what degree will research and execution become automated? We are already seeing a lot of companies innovating in that space, and it's absolutely another area where we are keeping our eyes open. It all goes back to hyper-personalization: AI will help advisors develop portfolios that are highly personalized and relevant for the goals of their clients, and they will be able to do that at scale for thousands of clients. There's a very big opportunity for AI there. The reason we haven't yet made a bet in this area is that it's very crowded, and we're trying to decide which company will likely emerge as a winner.

One more area where we feel gen AI can play a great role is compliance. Every entity that is regulated by SEC, Finra, or another regulator, whatever external communication they publish has to be compliant. We've invested in a company called Norm Ai. It has developed a tool whereby as you type — and it could be an investor relations document, a marketing document, or another type of document — Norm Ai tells you whether what you're typing is compliant. And if it's not, it can suggest how to rephrase your writing to make it more compliant. It goes back to making everybody more efficient at what they do.

The middle class is an underserved market. Do you think in the coming years we'll see solutions emerge that allow advisors to serve less-wealthy clients profitably? I would say yes, and we already have examples of companies that are doing that. It's not in our portfolio, but I would love to call out Range and what they've done. I think it's actually brilliant. They are using gen AI to democratize access to financial planning. Basically, they have built a whole back-end system, which is driven by gen AI, and they also have very different advisors on payroll, like wealth advisors, tax advisors, and estate advisors, and they're able to serve a high number of customers with the smaller number of advisors by leveraging these tools.

This is more targeted to younger generations, which are not that demanding in terms of speaking to an advisor. They want to do everything via chat, via text. They don't want to pick up the phone and talk to a person. Range is built in such a way that it can service and give you advice by leveraging technology and the expert that they have on hand without you having just one advisor. You basically tap into their pool of advisors, and the way they interact via technology makes them efficient. So there are definitely companies that are innovating in that space and democratizing access to financial planning. But I think high-touch, white-glove service and more digital, AI-driven firms can coexist.

You've probably seen the recent McKinsey study that projects a shortfall of 100,000 advisors by 2034. What kind of opportunities does that open for innovators? I think that presents a tremendous opportunity, and I think that shortfall can be solved by making advisors more efficient. How are we going to make them more efficient? By giving them the tools to help them spend less time on admin first and foremost. I see AI becoming like a sidekick to an advisor. In an ideal world, every advisor would have something like a mini desktop which has all these different tools plugged in, so they can just click and generate whatever they need or whatever the client needs, in a super-efficient, highly personalized manner. We are not there yet, but I think that is the future.

data.title: Boeing feels little economic pain as strike at defense unit head for ninth week
data.description:
Key points:
  • Boeing under less pressure now than during last year's strike
  • Union that worked on cash-cow jetliner won concessions in 2024
  • Boeing Defense says it is ready to hire replacement workers

By Dan Catchpole

A strike by machinists at Boeing Defense in St. Louis reached 53 days on Friday, matching last year’s walkout by fellow International Association of Machinists and Aerospace Workers union members in the Northwest who make most of the company's commercial jets.

Last year's strike ended with substantial concessions from Boeing.

This time around, management has refused to significantly improve upon offers already rejected by the roughly 3,200 machinists on strike since August 4 in the St. Louis area.

The union and the company are expected to resume contract negotiations on Monday with the help of a federal mediator, the union said.

Boeing faces far less economic pressure than it did during the 2024 strike because the current work stoppage largely affects military programs producing at low rates or still in testing rather than the company's cash-cow 737 jetliner program, AeroDynamic Advisory Managing Director Richard Aboulafia said.

He added that the U.S. government has already paid for much of the equipment and components for fighter jets, reducing the financial sting of parts sitting idle during the stoppage.

“It’s hard to see a pain point for Boeing,” he said. “They’re banking on outlasting the other guy, and they might just get away with it.”

For now, members are relying on a mix of $300 a week in strike benefits from the IAM, second jobs, and belt-tightening. The union sent Boeing a contract proposal that members overwhelmingly approved on September 19, according to union officials. Boeing leadership rejected any unilaterally proposed offer, the company told the union.

The company has warned it could hire replacement workers and is reviewing hundreds of applications after a job fair held last week, a spokesperson said.

Operations during the strike: Since the walkout began, Boeing has delivered one F/A-18 and one F-15 and produced 3,120 JDAM bomb-modification kits, Boeing spokeswoman Deborah VanNierop said.

Flight testing continues on the T-7 trainer and the MQ-25 tanker drone, which has completed ground-vibration testing and moved into fuel testing, she added. VanNierop declined to comment on union claims that no new work is being performed on the fighter programs.

Separately, Boeing’s head of labor relations, Michael Fitzsimmons, is leaving to take a similar role at Ford, which announced the move Thursday. His departure will not affect potential negotiations, VanNierop said.

“We’re not coming in here to play games. We’ve got too many people whose lives count on these jobs,” IAM Resident Vice President Jody Bennett said during a press briefing on Tuesday. “Right now, they’re without pay, they’re without insurance, and they’re without certainty of what’s going to happen in their future moving forward.”

data.title: Private equity owners of MRI Software target sale or IPO at $10 billion valuation, sources say
data.description:
Key points:
  • Goldman Sachs advising on potential sale or IPO in next 12 months
  • Deal would result in substantial profits for MRI's private equity backers
  • Company maintains 10% annual growth rate, over half of revenue from outside US

By Milana Vinn

The private equity owners of real estate software provider MRI Software are exploring options for the company, including a potential sale or a public listing on a U.S. exchange, according to people familiar with the matter.

Any deal for MRI Software, which could happen within the next 12 months, is expected to value the software maker at up to $10 billion including debt, the sources said, requesting anonymity as the discussions are confidential.

The ownership consortium includes TA Associates, Harvest Partners and GI Partners. It is working with Goldman Sachs on the process, which was kicked off by inbound acquisition interest from other real estate and technology firms, the sources added.

Spokespeople for MRI Software, TA Associates, GI Partners and Goldman declined to comment. Harvest Partners did not respond to a comment request.

Headquartered in Solon, Ohio, MRI provides software for the real estate industry that helps property owners, investors, and managers oversee commercial and residential portfolios. Among the software tools are those handling accounting, leasing, and financial management.

MRI Software is approaching $1 billion in annual revenue and generates approximately $400 million in earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA), according to the sources. The company has maintained a steady annual growth rate of 10%, with more than half of its revenue generated outside the U.S., the sources said.

A sale or listing at the purported valuation would represent a major exit for MRI Software's private equity backers. GI Partners, which invested in the company in 2015, is on track to return nine times their initial equity investment, while TA Associates, the largest holder of MRI, is projected to make seven times its money, the sources said.

Application software firms, including those in real estate, have recently seen a pickup in M&A, with private equity buyers paying attractive prices thanks to the steady recurring revenue these businesses generate.

Last month, Reuters reported that Altus Group , a Canadian software and data analytics provider focused on the real estate industry, is exploring a potential sale after receiving inbound acquisition interest.

data.title: US tells India that Russian oil curbs are key to trade deal progress
data.description:
By Jarrett Renshaw

U.S. trade negotiators have told their Indian counterparts that curbing purchases of Russian oil is crucial to reducing India's tariff rate and sealing a trade deal, two people familiar with the talks told Reuters.

While trade negotiations are on a positive track, a U.S. official said, more work is needed to ensure India addresses U.S. concerns over market access, the trade deficit and purchases of Russian oil.

U.S. President Donald Trump has sought to pressure India, the European Union and members of NATO to curb purchases of Russian oil as a way to cut Moscow’s revenue and hasten an end to the war in Ukraine.

The Trump administration has shown a willingness to use maximum leverage to advance its policy goals, as evidenced by it linking trade negotiations with India to demands that it curb Russian oil purchases.

Trump has expressed growing frustration over the slow progress toward ending Russia's war in Ukraine, a conflict he pledged to resolve from his first day in office.

The U.S. has imposed an extra 25% tariff on imports from India to pressure New Delhi to halt its purchases of discounted Russian crude oil, bringing total punitive duties on Indian goods to 50% and souring trade negotiations between the two democracies.

But Trump has refrained from imposing additional tariffs on Chinese imports over China's purchases of Russian oil, as his administration navigates a delicate trade truce with Beijing.

India and China are the two largest buyers of Russian oil, which is already subject to numerous U.S. sanctions that have constrained Moscow’s access to global markets.

In response, India has defended its oil imports, emphasizing the economic benefits and accusing Western nations of hypocrisy for continuing trade with Russia despite sanctions.
